mnemonics: People Always Store Secrets With Discretion (PASSWD)
Changing Passwords Secures Your Data (C-P-S-Y-D for the command structure)
Root Rules, Users Request (Only root can change other users' passwords directly)
keyPoints: The /etc/passwd file stores user account information, including username, UID, GID, home directory, and login shell.
The passwd command is used by users to change their own password, which is then stored in the /etc/shadow file.
The superuser (root) can change any user's password and modify the /etc/passwd file directly.
commonMistakes: Confusing /etc/passwd (user info) with /etc/shadow (encrypted passwords), thinking passwords are stored in passwd.
Forgetting that regular users can only change their own password, not others'.
Attempting to edit /etc/passwd directly without proper privileges or a tool like vipw, leading to file corruption.
